source_text: “Certainly, my dear, certainly,” replied the old gentleman. “Stay. There’s a pitcher of water in the corner by the door. Bring it here; and I’ll give you a basin to wash in, my dear.”
raw_output: “Certainly, my dear, certainly,” replied the old gentleman. “Wait. There’s a pitcher of water in the corner by the door. Bring it here, and I’ll give you a basin to wash in, my dear.”

source_text: The front door bell rang. The landlady answered it. Sarah left Gerard and Denys treed by a bear and listened. Oh, yes; you would, just as she did!

source_text: “Oho! won’t it?” thundered the champion of the root of evil. “You tell me where your exclusive circles would be if the first Astor hadn’t had the money to pay for his steerage passage over?”
raw_output: “Oho! won’t it?” roared the champion of the root of evil. “You tell me where your exclusive circles would be if the first Astor hadn’t had the money to pay for his steerage passage over?”

source_text: We present and evaluate a set of baseline models for the correction task (Section 2) in which we use SPLASH for training and testing (unless otherwise stated). Our main evaluation measure is the correction accuracy-the percentage of the testing set examples that are corrected-in which we follow Yu et al. (2018) and compare the corrected parse to the gold parse using exact set match. 6 We also report the end-to-end accuracy on Spider development set (which we use to construct our testing set) of the two turn interaction scenario: first Seq2Struct attempts to parse the input question. If it produced a wrong parse the question together with that parse and the corresponding feedback are attempted using the correction model. An example is considered "correct" if any of the two attempts produces the correct parse. 7
raw_output: We present and evaluate a set of baseline models for the correction task (Section 2), using SPLASH for both training and testing unless otherwise noted. Our primary evaluation metric is correction accuracy, namely the proportion of test examples that are corrected; following Yu et al. (2018), we compare the corrected parse with the gold parse by exact set match. 6 We also report end-to-end accuracy on the Spider development set, which we use to construct our test set, under a two-turn interaction setting: Seq2Struct first attempts to parse the input question. If it produces an incorrect parse, the question, together with that parse and the associated feedback, is then passed to the correction model. An example is counted as “correct” if either of the two attempts yields the correct parse. 7
rewrite_text: We present and evaluate a set of baseline models for the correction task (Section 2), using SPLASH for both training and testing unless otherwise noted. Our primary evaluation metric is correction accuracy, namely the proportion of test examples that are corrected; following Yu et al. (2018), we compare the corrected parse with the gold parse by exact set match. 6 We also report end-to-end accuracy on the Spider development set, which we use to construct our test set, under a two-turn interaction setting: Seq2Struct first attempts to parse the input question. If it produces an incorrect parse, the question, together with that parse and the associated feedback, is then passed to the correction model. An example is counted as “correct” if either of the two attempts yields the correct parse. 7

source_text: Early approaches to dialogue coherence modeling are built upon available models for monologue, such as the EntityGrid model Lapata, 2005, 2008) . EntityGrid and its extensions (Burstein et al., 2010; Guinaudeau and Strube, 2013; Mesgar and Strube, 2014; Tien Nguyen and Joty, 2017; Farag and Yannakoudakis, 2019) rely on entity transitions, as proxies of semantic connectivity, between utterances. These approaches are agnostic to discourse properties of dialogues (Purandare and Litman, 2008; Gandhe and Traum, 2008; Cervone et al., 2018) . He's a captain. inform utt4:
